text:
Teach For America (TFA) has managed to secure hundreds of millions of dollars in public and private funding to bring in what many claim are the brightest college graduates to teach for just two years in low-income urban and rural schools. Since its inception in 1990, the overall number of corps members has increased by about 2,000% on TFA’s home turf of Greater Houston. But whether the program has truly done more good than bad for disadvantaged children who need proper education is a matter of great controversy. On one side are people that believe the noble idea of Teach For America is a valid way to bring in the best and brightest talent to try to fill the gigantic voids that exist in the nation’s poorest schools. On the other side, however, are critics who argue that the entire program is nothing more than a short term way to provide funding for schools to temporarily cover openings in their staffs, and that it actually deprives those same schools of more legitimate forms of funding that would actually benefit the students that need the best training and teachers for years to come.

Critics of Teach for America (TFA) frequently cite non-peer-reviewed reports of alleged successes based on foundation funding of TFA and other related entities. However, the peer-reviewed literature on the effectiveness of teachers produced by the corps offers a more complicated and nuanced assessment of the effects of the program. The significance of the findings is highly dependent on whether corps members are in their residency or advanced teaching certification and also who the control group of veteran teachers in the study are. It is essential that TFA move away from being a program of uncertain influence toward being a program of proven effect in improving U.S. public schools. The authors offer recommendations to lawmakers and school administrators on how to carry out that transition.
